title: Cur Deus Homo
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 12-03-23 - AM- Cur Deus Homo - John 1_1-5 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Advent Question
subpoints: Advent provides opportunity to meditate on the incarnation
Anselm of Canterbury's Cur Deus Homo (1094 AD): Why God Became Man
The hypostatic union: one person, fully God and fully man
Without a right understanding of who Christ is, what He did becomes insignificant

outline.title: Jesus Is Fully Human
subpoints: The earliest heresies denied Christ's humanity (Docetism)
Physical world seen as bad in first-century culture — God becoming flesh seemed impossible
1 John 1:1-3: John appeals to his own senses — he heard, saw, and touched Jesus
John 1:14: the Word became flesh and dwelt among us
Jesus called Himself a man (John 8)
1 Corinthians 15:21: by man came death; by man also came resurrection
Jesus had a human body and human soul: hunger, thirst, weariness, grief, anger, love
After resurrection He invited touching and asked for food (Luke 24) — ghosts don't eat

outline.title: Jesus Is Fully God
subpoints: The modern struggle is denying His divinity, not His humanity
John 1:1: "In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God"
Jehovah's Witnesses argue for "a god" (no article) — but the same grammar applies to John 1:6 and 1:18
Isaiah 9:6: His name is "Mighty God, Everlasting Father"
Titus 2:13: the Granville Sharp rule — Jesus Christ is our great God and Savior
Romans 9:5: Christ is described as "overall, the eternally blessed God"
Jeremiah 23:5-6: the Branch from David is called "the LORD our righteousness" — Yahweh
John 8: "Before Abraham was, I AM" — the divine name; the Pharisees took up stones
John 18: "I am He" and the soldiers fell backward
God commands worship of Jesus — and God shares His worship with no one (therefore Jesus is God)
Thomas worships Jesus: "My Lord and my God" — Jesus accepts the worship

outline.title: Why Jesus Must Be Fully Human
subpoints: To represent us in life and in death
His obedience as a human is our righteousness
His death as a human is our death
The perfect sacrifice must be one of us (Hebrews 9-10: the blood of bulls and goats was insufficient)
The second Adam succeeds where the first Adam failed (1 Corinthians 15:21-22)

outline.title: Why Jesus Must Be Fully God
subpoints: So that God may receive all the glory in salvation
Romans 3: God's purpose — to be just AND the justifier
God alone could provide the God-man; salvation is entirely from the LORD
Jonah: "Salvation is from the LORD"
No man could bear the infinite weight of God's wrath in finite time
The smallest sin against an infinitely holy God incurs infinite punishment
Anselm: the chief motivation for the cross was not love or mercy — it was justice
God loves His own perfect character; He will never negotiate His justice

summary: Pastor Tito Lyro opens the Advent season by preaching from John 1:1-5 on the incarnation of Christ, framing the sermon around Anselm of Canterbury's eleventh-century treatise Cur Deus Homo — "Why God Became Man." He argues that the hardest thing about Christmas is not finding the perfect gift but understanding the necessity of a Savior who is both fully God and fully man — what theologians call the hypostatic union. He traces the earliest heresies: Docetism denied Christ's humanity because the physical world was considered evil in first-century thought. Today the more common error is denying His divinity. He establishes Christ's full humanity through John 1:14, 1 John 1:1-3, Jesus calling Himself "a man" in John 8, His physical experiences in the Gospels, and the resurrection appearances in Luke 24. He establishes Christ's full divinity through John 1:1 (refuting the Jehovah's Witness reading), Isaiah 9:6, Titus 2:13 (the Granville Sharp rule), Romans 9:5, Jeremiah 23:5-6 (the Branch called "Yahweh our righteousness"), the "I AM" statements of John 8 and 18, and the accepted worship of Thomas. He then explains why each nature is necessary for salvation: the humanity so Christ can represent us as the second Adam, obey in our place, die in our place, and be the perfect sacrifice; the divinity so that God alone receives the glory, so that God's justice is satisfied, and so that someone could bear the infinite weight of God's wrath for finite sin. Following Anselm and Sproul, he notes that the primary motivation for the incarnation and cross was not love or mercy but justice — God's unwillingness to negotiate His own perfect character.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: John 1:1-5

If you're able, do you remain standing for a minute longer as we read God's Word? I'll be reading from the Gospel of John, chapter 1, verses 1 through 5.

"In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God. He was in the beginning with God. All things were made through Him, and without Him nothing was made that was made. In Him was life, and the life was the light of men. And the light shines in the darkness, and the darkness did not comprehend it."

This is the Word of our Lord. Father, pray that You show Yourself strong in our weakness. I am not sufficient for these things, but Your Spirit is. So we pray that You open our eyes to see wonderful things concerning You in Your Word. In Jesus' name, amen. Please be seated.

## Introduction: The Advent Season

Christians throughout the world are starting the Advent season today. The Advent season is not mandated in the Scriptures — it is not something that a church must observe, and a Christian is not sinning if they don't pay attention to the Advent season. But we do know the Scriptures teach that Christ came and that the Son became flesh and dwelt among us. So this season provides us with the opportunity to think about the incarnation of God in order to save us from our sins. For the next few Sundays, we're going to take the opportunity to meditate on the incarnation of God the Son to save His people.

Every year we are inundated with commercials, ads, and emails telling us the things we must have in order to have a happy Christmas. According to Good Housekeeping Magazine, the hottest item for 2023 is the everywhere belt bag from Lululemon at $38. Amazon lists a mystery taco box as their leading item. The truth is that there are very few truly essential things for a happy Christmas. The most essential thing of all is a right understanding of the necessity of having a Savior who is both God and man.

One of the most important books in medieval Christianity was a book by Anselm of Canterbury called Cur Deus Homo — "Why God Man" — "Why God Became Man." He revolutionized thinking about how God saves people, explaining why it is so important to have a Savior who is both fully man and fully God. He did that all the way back in 1094 AD.

I want to show you today that a Savior is only a Savior if He is both God and man in one person. This teaching — the hypostatic union of the person of Christ — is perhaps the hardest teaching in theology to wrap our minds around. How can one person be both fully God — eternal and unchangeable in all His attributes — and at the same time a finite man? But even with its difficulty, this is just everyday Christianity.

## Jesus Christ Is Fully Human

The first thing I want us to see is that Jesus Christ our Savior is fully man — fully human, like you and like me.

The earliest of heresies concerning Christ involved the denial of His humanity. The early church had a hard time with the idea that God was fully man. First and second century thinking was contaminated with the idea that the material, physical world was essentially bad. So to have a Savior who is physical didn't make sense to them. When some teachers looked at Jesus and saw that He was essentially good, they automatically concluded that Jesus could not have been fully human — because physical is bad. So they would explain away the physicality of Jesus, often saying that Jesus appeared human but really wasn't. He was a spirit merely appearing to be a man. There were all kinds of different explanations, because they simply could not conceive the idea that God had become a person, a human like you and I.

We see this belief very early — in the first century — and the Apostle John wrote against it. If you read 1 John 1:1-3, you see how John appeals to his senses: "That which was from the beginning, which we have heard, which we have seen with our eyes, which we have looked upon and our hands have handled — concerning the Word of life — we have seen and heard and declare to you that you also may have fellowship with us." John says: what I'm declaring to you is the man Jesus Christ, the God in the flesh, fully human like you and I.

In 1 John 4, John says that if you don't believe that Christ the Son of God came in the flesh, you don't believe the true gospel of Jesus Christ.

So Jesus existed before the incarnation — and He became fully human at the incarnation. That's why John says in John 1:14: "The Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we beheld His glory, the glory as of the only begotten of the Father, full of grace and truth."

The Son existed before there was any hint of the first Christmas. But when Mary became pregnant for the first time, humanity was added to His person. At the moment of conception, the Son became human.

The Bible teaches us that Jesus was fully a man. He called himself a man. In John 8, speaking of himself, He says: "Now you seek to kill me, a man who has told you the truth." In 1 Corinthians 15:21-22, Paul says: "For since by man came death, by man also came the resurrection of the dead. For as in Adam all die, even so in Christ all shall be made alive."

If you read the Gospels, you see that Jesus possessed elements essential to humanity. He had a human body and a human soul. After the resurrection, if you look at Luke 24, He shows up and says "Peace be with you." They're all astounded. And one of the things He tells them is to touch Him — because He wanted them to understand He was not Casper the Friendly Ghost but actually a man come back from the dead. And in every account He asks them: "Do you have anything to eat?" Because ghosts don't eat; only people eat.

In John 11, when He saw Lazarus dead, He grieved in His soul. Jesus Christ Himself, having a body and soul, experienced normal human needs and emotions: hunger, thirst, weariness, confusion, grief, love, anger. Jesus also suffered and died — those are human experiences.

## Jesus Christ Is Fully God

In more recent centuries there hasn't been as much of a struggle believing that Jesus was fully human. Most people are willing to admit that. The problem more recently has been a struggle with admitting that He is also fully God — which is equally necessary for Him to be the Savior of mankind.

This is the teaching of both the Old and New Testaments. We saw it specifically in the text we read this morning, John 1:1-3: "In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God." And in verse 14, that Word became flesh — His name was Jesus.

If a Jehovah's Witness comes to your house and presents their gospel, and you say "We don't believe in the same Jesus — my Jesus is fully God" and you show them John 1:1, they will reply: "But there's no article there. Verse 1 should read 'the Word was a God' — lowercase g, a lesser God." They think they've got you.

But then you say: is that really how we are supposed to translate that? Because in verse 6 of the same chapter: "There was a man sent from God whose name was John." That expression "sent from God" uses the same construction as in verse 1. If you're going to say verse 1 means "a God," you'd have to say verse 6 means "a God" as well. And in verse 18: "No one has seen God at any time" — there's no article there either. So if you're going to follow the same logic in verse 1, you end up with multiple lesser gods, which is completely inconsistent with the Bible's consistent command to have no other gods besides the true God of Scripture, the Triune "I AM."

But besides John 1, there are many passages that speak of Jesus as God.

Isaiah 9:6: "For unto us a Child is born, unto us a Son is given; and the government will be upon His shoulder. And His name will be called Wonderful Counselor, Mighty God, Everlasting Father, Prince of Peace." Who is that? The Lord Jesus Christ.

Titus 2:13: "Looking for the blessed hope and glorious appearing of our great God and Savior Jesus Christ." The way that is written in Greek — the Granville Sharp rule — "great God and Savior" refers to the same person: Jesus Christ. A seminary education would show you that Titus 2:13 cannot grammatically mean anything else. You can say you don't believe it. You can say you don't like it. But you cannot say it doesn't mean that.

Romans 9:5: "Of whom are the fathers and from whom, according to the flesh, Christ came, who is over all, the eternally blessed God."

Jeremiah 23:5-6: "Behold, the days are coming, says the LORD, that I will raise to David a Branch of righteousness; a King shall reign and prosper, and execute judgment and righteousness in the earth. In His days Judah will be saved, and Israel will dwell safely. Now this is His name by which He will be called: THE LORD OUR RIGHTEOUSNESS." Who is that Branch from David, that descendant who will sit on the throne? Jesus Christ. And His name is Yahweh — "the LORD" — the name God revealed to Moses at the burning bush.

In the Gospel of John, you have the seven "I AM" statements — the bread of life, the living water, the door, the good shepherd, the resurrection and the life. But in chapter 8, He is debating the Pharisees about Abraham, and at the end of the chapter He says: "Before Abraham was, I AM." In Aramaic, He would have said the very word that was forbidden for a Jew to pronounce — the divine name. The Jews understood exactly what He was claiming, because they picked up stones to stone Him for blasphemy.

In John 18, when the soldiers came to arrest Him, He asked "Whom are you seeking?" They said "Jesus of Nazareth." And He said "I am He" — and the soldiers fell backward. They understood that "I AM" was the name of God.

The Bible commands us to worship Jesus. And God says He will not share His worship with anyone else. Put it together: Jesus is God. When Thomas, who had missed church the Sunday Jesus appeared and therefore hadn't seen Him, finally saw Jesus the following Sunday — he fell on his face and said: "My Lord and my God!" Those are words of worship. And Jesus did not say "Wait, wait, wait — I am not God, don't worship me" the way the angel in Revelation does. Jesus received that worship — because He is God.

When Jesus rode into Jerusalem on Palm Sunday and the children were singing Psalm 118, "Hosanna to God in the highest," the Pharisees said: "Make them stop worshiping you." And Jesus said: "If these children stop, the rocks will start worshiping me." He is that God, the God who created all things.

## Why Jesus Must Be Fully Human

These two truths — that Jesus is fully human and fully divine — make Him the perfect Savior. And they are each necessary.

Jesus must be fully man so that He can represent us in life and in death. Paul says in 1 Corinthians 15:21-22: "For since by man came death, by man also came the resurrection of the dead. For as in Adam all die — all that Adam represented, the human race — even so in Christ all, all that Christ represents, the elect of God, shall be made alive."

Christ had to obey God as a man — like us, as a human — so that His righteousness could be our righteousness. He had to die as a human so that His death could be our death. And in order to be the perfect sacrifice for humans, He had to be human. The book of Hebrews says the blood of bulls and goats wasn't sufficient. But the blood of one of us — the perfect One — accomplished what all the animal sacrifices could only point to.

He had to be fully human in order to succeed as the second Adam where the first Adam failed. The first Adam wasn't able to stand in righteousness. The second Adam — Jesus Christ — lived perfectly for us, died in our place, and rose for our justification.

## Why Jesus Must Be Fully God

Jesus must also be fully God. First, so that God may receive all the glory in salvation. In Romans 3, God explains why He did things the way He did — and He says He did it so that He might be just AND the justifier. The whole work of salvation is by God and by God alone, who provided the God-man Jesus Christ who could accomplish all that needed to be accomplished for our salvation. As Jonah discovered in the belly of the whale: "Salvation is from the LORD."

Second, Jesus must be fully God so that God's justice would be satisfied. No one else could bear infinite punishment in a finite time. This is, in a sense, a simple math issue: the smallest sin we commit is against an infinitely holy God — and because of that, it incurs infinite punishment. And yet Jesus took that infinite punishment on the cross in the span of a Friday afternoon. Only God could do that. No mere man could bear the weight of God's wrath all at once.

This brings us full circle back to Anselm's Cur Deus Homo. R.C. Sproul, commenting on Anselm, said: "At the heart of Anselm's answer to that question was his understanding of the character of God. Anselm saw that the chief reason the God-man was necessary was the justice of God. That may seem to be a strange answer, thinking of the cross and of Christ's atonement — we assume that what most strangely motivated God to send Christ into the world was His love or His mercy. As a result, we tend to overlook the characteristic of God's nature that makes this atonement absolutely necessary — His justice."

It wasn't primarily His love. It wasn't primarily His mercy. It was His justice that sent Jesus Christ — the God-man — to live, die, and come back to life for you and for me.

"God is loving, but a major part of what He loves is His own perfect character, with a major aspect being the importance of maintaining justice and righteousness. Though God pardons sinners and makes great provision for expressing His mercy, He will never negotiate His justice. If we fail to understand that, the cross of Christ will be utterly meaningless to us."

If we forget that we have a God who is just and was highly offended by our sin, the cross of Christ means nothing to us — it's just something nice.

## Closing Application

So the hardest thing about Christmas is not a gift from Lululemon. The hardest thing about Christmas is Jesus Christ — the God-man who came to save His people.

You might say: "Okay, Pastor, that's fine theology — but I'm struggling. My marriage is falling apart. My kids are struggling. I have all kinds of troubles."

This is where the solution for your struggles begins — that God sent Himself to save you, to liberate you, to free you from the dominion of sin, to give you heaven itself, to give Himself to you, that you might be freed by His grace to live righteously for His glory.

Your marriage is struggling. Jesus Christ, the God-man — fully God, fully man — came for you. Your kids are rebelling. Jesus Christ, the God-man — fully God, fully man — came for you. It is through Him that life makes sense. It is through Him that this life, as we are on the way to the life to come, works together for the good of His people.

So why God became man — so that you and I could be saved and spend eternity with our God.

Let us pray together. God, we thank you that you are a God who is about Yourself, and we praise you for that. We thank you that you are a just God, and in your justice you have provided salvation for us through the Lord Jesus Christ — fully God, fully man. It is a difficult concept for us to understand, God, but your Bible teaches it. We pray that we embrace it and see the significance of it. Father, we pray that you be with those who don't believe in you in our midst today. We pray to save them. Pray to open their eyes to see the glory of the God-man, the Lord Jesus Christ, that they would embrace Him and His cross. In His name, amen.
